serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of crab meat omelette is approximately 200 g, comprising a pan-fried egg omelette filled with seasoned crab meat, served as a main dish or shared side at a Chinese seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Egg omelette with seafood — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet (FDC ID 173424) and Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 171975), combined in estimated proportions typical of a Chinese crab omelette (60% egg, 30% crab meat, 10% oil and seasonings)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for crab meat omelette found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by combining USDA FDC data for plain omelette (FDC ID 173424) and blue crab cooked moist heat (FDC ID 171975) in proportions of approximately 60% egg omelette and 30% crab meat with 10% oil and seasoning contribution. Energy and macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA egg dish analogues. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg-based and seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on plain omelette / egg dish GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; eggs and crab meat are predominantly protein and fat with negligible glycemic carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for crab meat omelette. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of egg-based dishes as low GI (GI < 20) due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~1.8 per 100g). Glycemic load per 200g serving = (15 × 3.6g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.54, rounded to 0.5. This is consistent with the low-GI classification of protein- and fat-dominant egg dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100g; ~760 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with crab meat omelette as a single 200g restaurant serving may contribute approximately 760 mg sodium — about one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — particularly when soy sauce and seasoning are used liberally; requesting reduced soy sauce or sharing the dish can help manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal carbohydrate content (~1.8g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crab meat omelette is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index and negligible carbohydrate content, producing a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving; the high protein content (~12.8g per 100g) further supports postprandial blood glucose stability, though total dietary fat and cholesterol should be monitored in the context of the overall meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~295 mg per 100g) and saturated fat (~2.5g per 100g) from eggs and crab
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume crab meat omelette in moderation; a 200g serving provides approximately 590 mg dietary cholesterol — nearly double the historically recommended daily limit — and while current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals, the combination of egg yolk cholesterol and saturated fat warrants portion control and infrequent consumption as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Meat Omelette (螃蟹肉煎蛋) is a Chinese-style egg dish popular in Singapore seafood restaurants, consisting of beaten whole eggs pan-fried or wok-fried in oil until golden and fluffy, filled or topped with seasoned crab meat — typically blue swimmer crab or mud crab — and flavoured with soy sauce, sesame oil, spring onion, and white pepper. The texture is soft and custardy on the inside with lightly crisp edges, and the flavour is savoury, umami-rich, and mildly briny from the crab. It is commonly served as a shared main dish or side at Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~12.8g per 100g) and moderate in fat (~9.6g per 100g), with very low carbohydrate content (~1.8g per 100g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI ~15) suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is a notable source of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and cholesterol from both eggs and crab. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is not suitable for those with crustacean shellfish or egg allergies, and its halal status depends on the restaurant's certification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crab meat omelette?
Is crab omelette good for diabetics?
Is crab omelette high in cholesterol?
Can I eat crab omelette if I have high blood pressure?
What allergens are in crab meat omelette?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crab meat omelette was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FDC data for plain omelette (FDC ID 173424) and cooked blue crab (FDC ID 171975) in proportions representative of a typical Chinese crab omelette preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (plain omelette FDC 173424 + blue crab cooked FDC 171975) in approximate proportions of 60% egg omelette, 30% crab meat, 10% oil and seasoning. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was found for this dish. Sodium value (~380 mg/100g) reflects moderate soy sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may be lower. Cholesterol value (~295 mg/100g) is derived primarily from egg yolk content. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not directly measured for this dish. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate for an egg-dominant dish with negligible glycemic carbohydrate, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish classifications. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (15 × 3.6g) / 100 = 0.54, rounded to 0.5. The cuisine_tags in the input included 'Lobster' which does not apply to this dish and has been disregarded. Food group retained as 'Dairy and eggs' per input, consistent with the egg-dominant nature of the dish. Halal status is marked false as crab is considered non-halal in some Islamic jurisprudence schools; restaurant-specific certification should be verified.
